articles.title: CACHING
articles.summary: Caching involves using faster, smaller storage to hold frequently accessed data, reducing latency. This article goes over various caching concepts such as hit rate, temporal and spatial locality, geospatial considerations, and different replacement policies like FIFO and LRU. It also touches on how databases like Postgres and MySQL implement caching internally.

articles.title: LEVERAGING ELIXIR'S HOT CODE LOADING CAPABILITIES TO MODULARIZE A MONOLITHIC APP
articles.summary: Elixir's hot code loading can be used to create client-specific features within a monolithic application fast. This approach involves dynamically loading and compiling client-specific LiveView apps at runtime, isolating client code during the main app's build process. By removing client-specific code during build time, it enforces dependencies and makes testing easier.

articles.title: FORGET BORROW CHECKERS: C3 SOLVED MEMORY LIFETIMES WITH SCOPES
articles.summary: The C3 language has a Temp allocator, a region-based memory management system that simplifies memory handling. This allocator automatically resets when execution leaves its scope, preventing memory leaks without needing manual cleanup or garbage collection. By tying memory lifetimes to scopes, C3 is able to have efficient, performant memory management without the complexity of traditional methods like reference counting.

articles.title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IS A COMMODITY, BUT UNDERSTANDING IS A SUPERPOWER
articles.summary: While AI is increasingly accessible, the ability to understand and effectively apply it is still uncommon. AI can generate code, but it doesn't have the human capacity to fully understand project goals, system complexities, and the deeper intention behind the code. Therefore, devs who have a strong understanding of both technical implementation and strategic purpose will be the most valuable.

articles.title: PARSE, DON'T VALIDATE AKA SOME C SAFETY TIPS
articles.summary: Instead of repeatedly validating input throughout the system, it's better to parse untrusted input into specific, opaque data types (like `email_t` and `name_t`) at the system's boundary. This makes sure that internal functions only handle well-defined, structured data, reducing attack surfaces and preventing misuse of raw character pointers.

articles.title: TAILWIND IS THE WORST FORM OF CSS, EXCEPT FOR ALL THE OTHERS
articles.summary: Tailwind CSS has drawbacks like verbose class names and a learning curve, but it is still great for teams. Its opinionated nature enforces consistency and reduces decision fatigue.

articles.title: >_ CLAUDE CODE LETS YOU UNLEASH THE RAW POWER OF CLAUDE OPUS 4 DIRECTLY IN YOUR TERMINAL
articles.summary: Run Claude Opus 4—Anthropic's most powerful model—directly in your environment, without leaving the terminal. Search million-line codebases [10]. Create new functionality. Run tests. Build faster than you ever dreamed you could build. Install NodeJS 18+ and then run

articles.title: MVPBLOCKS
articles.summary: MVPBlocks is an open-source, developer-first component library built with Next.js and Tailwind CSS. It has reusable UI blocks and CLI support to help devs quickly launch MVPs with customizable and copyable code.

articles.title: KIBO
articles.summary: Kibo is a custom registry of accessible and open-source components to enhance shadcn/ui. It wraps complex logic from various headless libraries.

articles.title: LANGFAST - PROMPT PLAYGROUND
articles.summary: LangFast is a prompt playground that allows users to test, share, and save prompt templates using Jinja2 templates and variables without API abstractions. It offers instant access with no signup required and a customizable workspace.

articles.title: GOOGLE'S REAPS THE REWARDS AS OPENAI'S DEAL TO ACQUIRE WINDSURF COLLAPSES
articles.summary: OpenAI's planned $3 billion acquisition of AI coding startup Windsurf collapsed after Anthropic cut off Windsurf's access to its Claude models, creating an opening for Google to swoop in. Google hired Windsurf's CEO, co-founder, and key researchers for $2.4 billion through an "acquihire" deal that avoids regulatory scrutiny while still getting talent and technology licensing without taking equity in the company.

articles.title: HOW I FOUND A BYPASS IN GOOGLE'S BIG ANTI-ADBLOCK UPDATE
articles.summary: This dev discovered a bug in Google Chrome that allowed MV3 extensions to bypass the intended restrictions on `webRequestBlocking`, a feature necessary for adblockers that Google was phasing out. The bug exploited a loophole in how Chrome handled WebView IDs within the `webRequest` API, allowing extensions to spoof permissions and reinstate the blocking functionality.

articles.title: SWITCHING TO CLAUDE CODE + VSCODE INSIDE DOCKER
NEW DATE("WTF")
articles.summary: A 28-question quiz on JavaScript's Date class.

articles.title: ZIG'S NEW ASYNC I/O
articles.summary: Zig's new asynchronous I/O interface allows devs to inject I/O implementations, express concurrency for potential parallelism, and support cancellation.

articles.title: HOW DOES A SCREEN WORK?
articles.summary: An overview of the history of digital displays, from CRTs to modern LCD and OLED technologies, with explanations of the underlying hardware principles and trade-offs involved in creating the images we see on our screens.

articles.title: OPENAI DELAYS OPEN-WEIGHT MODEL LAUNCH
articles.summary: The planned launch of OpenAI's open-weight model has been delayed indefinitely to allow for further safety testing and risk review due to the irreversible nature of releasing the model.
